Netherlands.group_record: 1D-2W, 7pts, GD+6 (D2-2 JPN, W5-1 SWE, W3-1 TUN)
Netherlands.status: Mild favorite (40.3% straight win, 58.8% to qualify) — not dominant

Morocco.group_record: 1D-2W, 7pts, GD+3 (D1-1 BRA, W1-0 SCO, W4-2 HAI)
Morocco.status: Fully competitive underdog (26.6% straight win, 41.8% to qualify)

NED.LESSON_TUN_NED: tun_ned Q2 (both teams 2H SOT) cost -18.24 RBP because Tunisia's '0 SOT vs Japan' one-match crisis suddenly broke against a Netherlands team that eased off after getting a lead. Morocco is a fundamentally different opponent — they genuinely threaten. Do NOT extrapolate 'weaker team SOT crisis continues' logic from Tunisia to Morocco.

MAR.BIMODAL_FLAG: Morocco's Haiti game (11 SOT, 5 offsides, 9 corners) is a clear bimodal outlier — in the other two competitive matches (vs Brazil and Scotland, both quality opponents), they had only 3 and 2 SOT, 1 and 0 offsides, 2 and 5 corners. True competitive-match attacking baseline ≈ 2-3 SOT, 0-1 offsides, 2-5 corners. Applied this for the offside lambda estimate (used 0.5, not 2.0 average, for Morocco's contribution).
MAR.SAIBARI_DIRECT_PRECEDENT: Ismael Saibari scored in BOTH GD1 (21' vs Brazil) AND GD2 (2' vs Scotland — earliest WC winning goal in a 1-0 in WC history). Brahim Diaz assisted both. Morocco's entire attack routes through this player; the live market mid-price (0.29 from bid/offer) is credible but potentially conservative given this documented streak. Score-OR-assist is a wider net than just scoring.

context_flags: GENUINELY_COMPETITIVE_MATCHUP: unlike earlier knockout blowouts (GER-PAR, BRA-JPN), this is a near-coin-flip in each direction — Netherlands only 40.3% to win in regulation, Morocco 26.6%, Draw 31.5%. The high draw price, 47% ET probability from 'No goal' at 9.27%, and Morocco's documented ability to absorb pressure (0-1 vs Scotland clean sheet, 1-1 vs Brazil) make this a legitimately open contest.

MOROCCO_BIMODAL_ATTACKING_OUTPUT: Morocco's attacking stats swing massively with opponent quality — Haiti game was a blowout outlier. Against true opposition (Brazil, Scotland), they showed only 2-3 SOT and 0-1 offsides, consistent with a defensive-first team that scores from tight setpieces/transitions rather than volume. Applied this for the offside Q (not asked today) and for calibrating Saibari's role.

SAIBARI_2_FOR_2_SCORING_STREAK: both WC2026 goals scored by Saibari, both in the first half, both early — an unusually concentrated, verifiable pattern in a small sample. Reflected as a modest upward bump from the liquid market mid on his 'score or assist' price.

LOSS_PATTERN_8_APPLIED_MORE_CONSERVATIVELY: Q2 (card after 2nd break + ET) uses the same self-constructed timing-decomposition methodology that produced our worst RSA-CAN result (-39.68 RBP) — applied the calculation but trimmed the output much more aggressively than RSA-CAN to reflect genuine uncertainty in the 40%-post-69' assumption that has never been independently verified.

TUN_NED_GAKPO_LESSON: liquid player market missed in TUN-NED (0.66 failed, Netherlands won without Gakpo directly involved). Applied here as a cautionary reminder that even a liquid, well-priced individual attacker can under-deliver when a TEAM win comes from distributed contributions — kept Brobbey estimate anchored to the market rather than inflating on narrative.

questions.text: Will Morocco be ahead at halftime?
questions.reasoning: Direct HT result market: Morocco = 23.02%. Despite being the regulation underdog, Morocco's defensive, low-block approach and Saibari's pattern of early goals means they're a real HT-lead threat — the market correctly prices this higher than a simple 'underdog' discount would suggest.

questions.text: Will a card be shown after the second hydration break, including any extra time?
questions.pick_tier: MEDIUM CONFIDENCE — TIMING-MODEL, LOSS-PATTERN-8 DISCOUNT APPLIED
questions.reasoning: Total card lambda backed out from two card O/U thresholds (OU1.5 Over=0.844, OU3.5 Over=0.474): avg λ≈3.44. Assuming 40% of cards land post-69', gives λ_post69≈1.38, P(≥1 card in late regulation)=0.75. Adding ET-card probability (18.99% chance of ET × 55% card in ET if no prior late card), raw result ≈0.77. Trimmed more aggressively than RSA-CAN (where same method hit -39.68 RBP) for the timing-split assumption uncertainty — Loss Pattern #8 is directly in memory.

questions.text: Will a goal be scored in first-half stoppage time?
questions.reasoning: Very specific narrow time window (~4 minutes, 45'+1 to 45'+4). Using total goal lambda (~2.43 from O/U2.5/O/U3.5 markets), scaled to 4/94 of match time, gives P(≥1 goal in this window)≈0.10. This is a genuinely rare event type — no floor convention applies here (this is a specific time-window binary, not a count-stat threshold). Both teams are competitive and dangerous, but the absolute window is narrow.

questions.text: Will a goal be scored after the second hydration break?
questions.reasoning: Using total goal lambda (~2.43), scaled to the post-69' window (~25 minutes of a ~94-minute match), gives P(≥1 goal from ~69' onward)=0.48. This is a broader, more achievable window than Q3, and the match's competitive, open nature (BTTS 53%) makes late goals structurally plausible.

questions.text: Will a red card be shown in the match?
questions.reasoning: Direct market: Any player to be sent off = 13.13%. Consistent with both teams' clean disciplinary records throughout the group stage (Netherlands 0 RC in 3 matches, Morocco 0 RC in 3 matches — unusually clean combination).

questions.text: Will the second half produce more goals than the first half in regulation, excluding extra time?
questions.reasoning: Direct market: Highest scoring half, Second Half = 42.81%.

questions.text: Will the Netherlands score the first goal of the match?
questions.reasoning: Direct market: Team to score first goal, Netherlands = 52.02%. Note: 'No goal' at 9.27% is unusually high — reflecting a real probability this match stays scoreless deep into regulation (consistent with Morocco's defensive profile and draw probability of 31.5%).

questions.text: Will Brian Brobbey have 2 or more shots on target in regulation?
questions.reasoning: Genuinely liquid market (bid 0.2041, offer 0.2174) — mid = 0.2108. Trusted directly, per TUN-NED Gakpo lesson: even a correctly-liquid individual attacker prop can under-deliver when the team wins through distributed rather than concentrated contributions. No inflation beyond market mid.

questions.text: Will the Netherlands have more corner kicks than Morocco in regulation?
questions.reasoning: Direct market: Netherlands -0.5 corners handicap (Netherlands wins the corner count) = 51.74%. The market already discounts Morocco's bimodal corner spike (9 vs Haiti) in favor of their true competitive-match corner output of 2-5.

questions.text: Will the Netherlands win in regulation?
questions.reasoning: Direct regulation-scoped market: Full-time result Netherlands = 40.32%. Notably modest for a team traditionally considered a top-tier football nation — the market fully reflects Morocco's proven competitive quality across this tournament.

questions.text: Will Ismael Saibari score or assist a goal (excluding own goals) in regulation?
questions.pick_tier: MEDIUM-HIGH CONFIDENCE — LIQUID MARKET, SMALL LESSON-BASED BUMP
questions.reasoning: Liquid market mid (bid 0.2326, offer 0.3401) = 0.2864. Bumped slightly above the market mid to reflect Saibari's documented 2-for-2 scoring streak this tournament (21' vs Brazil, 2' vs Scotland), Brahim Diaz's corresponding 2-for-2 assist record to him, and the 'score or assist' bar being wider than just a goal. He is clearly Morocco's primary attacking channel regardless of opponent quality.

questions.text: Will both teams score in regulation?
questions.reasoning: Direct market: BTTS Yes = 52.98%. Consistent with both teams' quality and Morocco's proven ability to find the goal against strong opposition (Brazil, Scotland).

questions.text: Will both teams receive at least one card in regulation?
questions.pick_tier: MEDIUM-HIGH CONFIDENCE — MODELED FROM TEAM-LEVEL CARD MARKETS
questions.reasoning: No direct 'both teams carded' market. Backed out individual team card lambdas from team O/U1.5 card markets (NED P(≥2 cards)=54.07% → λ_NED≈1.52 → P(≥1 card)=0.78; MAR P(≥2 cards)=51.80% → λ_MAR≈1.46 → P(≥1 card)=0.77). P(both) ≈ 0.78×0.77=0.60. Trimmed slightly for model uncertainty.

questions.text: Will the first half produce 2 or more goals?
questions.reasoning: Direct market: First half Over1.5 goals = 27.22%.

questions.text: Will a penalty kick be awarded during regulation?
questions.reasoning: Direct market: Penalty to be awarded = 22.73%.

strongest_picks: Q5: Red card in match = 0.13 (HIGH CONFIDENCE — direct market, consistent with both teams' unusually clean disciplinary records: 0 RC each in all 3 group matches)
Q3: Goal in first-half stoppage time = 0.10 (MEDIUM CONFIDENCE — modeled, genuinely rare narrow-window event)
Q11: Saibari score or assist = 0.32 (MEDIUM-HIGH — liquid market with documented 2-for-2 goal streak + 'score or assist' wider bar than pure goalscorer)

post_match_results.note: BEST MATCH OF THE SEASON SO FAR. Confirms this was a genuinely competitive, even fixture as modeled — the FTR-driven near-coin-flip framing was directly vindicated by the 1-1 regulation draw.

Q2.question: Card after 2nd hydration break, incl ET?
Q2.notes: Same timing-decomposition methodology as RSA-CAN's worst-ever result (-39.68), but after deliberately trimming the raw model output (0.77->0.65) per Loss Pattern #8, the miss was only -3.51 — direct evidence the correction worked, even though the direction was still slightly off. Validates: keep applying humility discounts to self-constructed timing models.

Q3.question: Goal in 1H stoppage time?
Q3.notes: Narrow-time-window Poisson scaling (no floor applied, correctly) beat a crowd that overpriced a rare event.

Q8.question: Brobbey 2+ SOT?
Q8.notes: BIGGEST WIN. Trusted the genuinely liquid market (mid 0.2108) directly per the TUN-NED Gakpo-lesson caution against narrative inflation — crowd over-trusted a 'two strong attacking teams' framing and priced Brobbey far too high. Liquid two-sided markets deserve direct trust over crowd narrative.

Q10.question: NED wins in regulation?
Q10.notes: Direct FTR market (40.32%) beat a crowd that over-rated Netherlands' traditional pedigree over their actual measured form.

Q13.question: Both teams carded?
Q13.notes: 2ND-BIGGEST WIN. Team-level card-lambda decomposition (0.60 raw, trimmed to 0.57) beat a crowd that over-trusted a 'two physical/competitive teams' narrative into assuming both would certainly be carded — disciplined, modeled decomposition beat narrative-driven crowd pricing.
